enrichment.short_description: Frontegg is a customer identity and access management (CIAM) platform that gives B2B SaaS companies drop-in authentication, authorization, multi-tenancy, and user management so engineering teams do not have to build identity infrastructure from scratch.
enrichment.starting_price: Free up to 7,500 monthly active users
enrichment.seo_title: Frontegg Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Frontegg review covering CIAM features, SSO and SCIM, multi-tenancy, 2026 pricing tiers, pros and cons, and how it compares to Auth0, WorkOS, and Clerk.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Frontegg is a customer identity and access management platform built specifically for business-to-business SaaS applications. Founded in 2019 by Sagi Rodin and Aviad Mizrachi, the company set out to solve a problem every growing SaaS vendor eventually hits: authentication, authorization, multi-tenant user management, and enterprise-readiness features like single sign-on and SCIM provisioning are expensive and time-consuming to build in-house, yet customers increasingly demand them as table stakes before signing a contract. Rather than positioning itself as a consumer identity provider, Frontegg focused from day one on the B2B use case, where a single application often needs to serve many customer organizations (tenants), each with its own users, roles, permissions, and security requirements. Frontegg is headquartered in Ramat Gan, near Tel Aviv, Israel, where its engineering and product organization is based, while its go-to-market and sales functions are concentrated around Mountain View, California, giving the company a foothold in both the Israeli tech ecosystem and the core US SaaS buyer market.

Frontegg has raised approximately 70 million dollars across multiple funding rounds from investors including Insight Partners, Stripes, Pitango, Global Founders Capital, and i3 Partners. Its most widely reported round was a 25 million dollar raise led by Insight Partners in December 2021, which the company used to expand its engineering team and push further into the enterprise segment of the CIAM market. Since then, Frontegg has continued to add capabilities around AI-generated identity workflows, branded and hosted login experiences, and expanded entitlement and subscription management tooling, positioning itself less as a pure authentication vendor and more as a full identity and monetization layer for SaaS applications. The company today employs roughly 70 to 80 people across offices on multiple continents, a relatively lean team compared to some of its larger rivals, reflecting its focus on a developer-first, API-driven product rather than a large enterprise sales organization.

Architecturally, Frontegg is delivered as a set of hosted APIs, SDKs, and pre-built UI components that development teams embed into their own applications. The platform separates into Management APIs, which require environment-level authorization and give administrators full control over SSO configurations, roles, permissions, and tenant settings, and Self-Service APIs, which are accessible via a user's own JWT and let end users manage their own account, security, and SSO settings without involving the vendor's support team. Core building blocks include a hosted, fully customizable login experience; multi-tenant user and organization management; role-based access control with fine-grained permissions; SSO support for both SAML and OpenID Connect so customers can connect identity providers like Okta, Azure AD, and Google Workspace; and SCIM-based user provisioning that keeps a customer's directory automatically synced with their app. Frontegg also ships an Entitlements module that ties subscription plans and feature flags directly to the identity layer, allowing SaaS vendors to gate features by plan tier without maintaining a separate billing-to-permissions mapping. More recently the company introduced Frontegg Flows, described as AI-generated identity workflows that let teams assemble multi-step authentication and onboarding journeys without hand-coding each step.

Frontegg's pricing follows a Pay-as-you-Go model that starts at zero dollars per month and includes a fully customized hosted login, 7,500 monthly active users, five enterprise SSO or SCIM connections, unlimited organizations, and a custom domain. Beyond the free allotment, usage-based charges apply based on metrics such as monthly active users, additional enterprise connections, and machine-to-machine tokens, though Frontegg does not publish exact per-unit overage rates; teams with meaningful usage typically end up paying from roughly 49 dollars per month upward depending on their MAU count and connection needs. For organizations that need advanced fraud protection, multiple isolated environments, a 99.99 percent uptime SLA, premium support, and access to add-ons, Frontegg offers an Enterprise tier that requires contacting sales for a custom quote. This two-tier structure, a generous free entry point paired with negotiated enterprise pricing, is designed to let early-stage SaaS companies adopt Frontegg at effectively no cost and only start paying meaningfully once their own product has scaled to a comparable number of paying users.

The platform is aimed squarely at engineering and product teams building B2B and B2B2C SaaS applications who need enterprise-readiness features, SSO, SCIM, audit logs, granular RBAC, without diverting scarce engineering resources away from their core product. Typical adopters range from early-stage startups that want a credible answer the first time a prospective enterprise customer asks about SSO, through to mid-market and growth-stage SaaS companies managing dozens or hundreds of tenant organizations, each potentially with different security policies, identity providers, and user hierarchies. Because Frontegg handles multi-tenancy as a first-class concept rather than an add-on, it is particularly well suited to vertical SaaS and platform businesses where each customer organization needs its own isolated user base, roles, and permission structure.

Frontegg competes in a crowded CIAM and developer identity market. Auth0, now part of Okta, is a longer-established and more consumer-oriented option, historically strong in B2C authentication but requiring more custom engineering to achieve deep multi-tenant B2B patterns. WorkOS focuses narrowly and efficiently on adding enterprise-readiness, SSO and directory sync, to an existing homegrown auth system, appealing to teams that do not want to replace their whole identity stack. Clerk differentiates with polished, pre-built UI components and a strong developer experience aimed at fast-moving startups, and has grown into the largest of these venture-backed identity companies by headcount. FusionAuth, a self-hostable alternative, appeals to teams that want to own their identity data outright, especially in regulated industries. Frontegg's differentiation is its combination of full multi-tenant CIAM depth, SSO, SCIM, RBAC, entitlements, and subscription-aware permissions, delivered through both hosted and highly customizable interfaces, with a free tier generous enough to support real production traffic before a company needs to negotiate a contract.

Within the identity infrastructure category, Frontegg sits between fully managed, opinionated platforms and lower-level toolkits that require more assembly. It has carved out a reputation among mid-market B2B SaaS vendors as a credible, faster-to-implement alternative to building identity in-house or wrestling with a heavier enterprise-only vendor like Okta or Ping Identity. Its relatively small team size compared to funding raised suggests a capital-efficient, product-led growth motion, and its continued investment in AI-assisted workflow tooling signals an attempt to stay ahead of newer entrants targeting the same developer audience. For directory purposes, Frontegg is best understood as a specialized B2B identity and access management layer rather than a general authentication library, most valuable to teams that specifically need multi-tenant SSO, SCIM, and entitlement management rather than simple username and password login.

faqs.question: Is Frontegg free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Frontegg's Pay-as-you-Go plan is free up to 7,500 monthly active users and includes hosted login, five enterprise SSO/SCIM connections, unlimited organizations, and a custom domain; usage beyond that is billed based on MAU and connection volume.

faqs.question: Does Frontegg support SAML and SCIM?
faqs.answer: Yes, Frontegg supports both SAML and OpenID Connect for single sign-on and SCIM for automated user provisioning and deprovisioning from identity providers like Okta, Azure AD, and Google Workspace.

faqs.question: Who founded Frontegg?
faqs.answer: Frontegg was founded in 2019 by Sagi Rodin and Aviad Mizrachi.

faqs.question: How is Frontegg different from Auth0?
faqs.answer: Frontegg is built specifically around B2B multi-tenancy, entitlements, and subscription-aware permissions, while Auth0 originated as a more general-purpose, consumer-oriented identity provider that typically requires more custom engineering to model multi-tenant B2B hierarchies.

faqs.question: Can Frontegg be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: No, Frontegg is delivered as a hosted, managed CIAM service accessed through APIs and SDKs; teams that require full self-hosting typically consider alternatives like FusionAuth or Keycloak instead.

faqs.question: What is Frontegg Flows?

enrichment.short_description: FullEnrich is a B2B contact data waterfall enrichment platform that combines 15-plus data providers to find and verify prospects' work emails and mobile phone numbers for sales and recruiting teams.
enrichment.starting_price: Free trial (50 credits); paid plans from $29/month
enrichment.headquarters: Paris, France and San Francisco, US (fully remote team)
enrichment.seo_title: FullEnrich Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: FullEnrich review: waterfall enrichment features, credit pricing, integrations, pros, cons, and how it compares to Clay, Apollo, and ZoomInfo.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
FullEnrich is a business-to-business contact enrichment platform that specializes in waterfall enrichment, a technique that queries many third-party data providers in sequence or in parallel for a single contact and returns the first or best verified result, rather than relying on one proprietary database the way many competitors do. The company was founded in 2023 by Paul Seloriot and Benjamin Douablin, with Douablin serving as CEO; Douablin's background includes service in the French special forces before moving into the sales technology space, a biography that has featured prominently in founder interviews about the company's origin story. FullEnrich was built around a simple observation that sales and recruiting teams were increasingly relying on multiple point-solution enrichment tools, such as ZoomInfo, Apollo, and Hunter, and stitching results together manually to maximize contact hit rates; FullEnrich instead automates that stitching process by simultaneously calling more than 15 underlying data sources per contact and returning the most accurate result it can find.

The company operates as a remote-first team with a dual base in Paris, France and San Francisco, California, and has grown from a small founding team to somewhere in the range of 20 to 60 employees as of 2026, depending on the data source consulted, reflecting a fast early growth trajectory. FullEnrich raised a 2 million dollar seed round, announced in February 2025, led by Flex Capital with participation from M-Fund, Kima Ventures, First Degree, Offord Ventures, and Duro Ventures. Public revenue estimates place the company in the low single-digit millions of dollars in annual recurring revenue as of 2024-2025, consistent with a young, capital-efficient startup rather than a heavily funded enterprise vendor.

Technically, FullEnrich functions less as a proprietary data owner and more as an orchestration layer on top of other providers' data assets. When a user submits a list of contacts, typically exported from LinkedIn Sales Navigator as a CSV, uploaded from a CRM, or pushed via API, FullEnrich runs a waterfall process that checks multiple sources in sequence for each contact's work email, personal email, and mobile phone number, only charging the user's account when a data point is actually found and verified. This pay-only-for-what-is-found model is central to FullEnrich's pitch: unlike flat per-seat enrichment tools, users are not charged for failed lookups. The platform integrates natively with HubSpot, Clay, Google Sheets, and Make, and connects to more than 1,000 additional apps through Zapier, alongside a documented REST API for custom integrations into internal tooling or outbound sales sequencers. Because LinkedIn discontinued third-party Chrome extension access to Sales Navigator in June 2024, FullEnrich's LinkedIn workflow today relies on CSV export and URL-based input rather than a live browser extension, a change that affected most enrichment vendors in the category simultaneously.

FullEnrich prices its product on a credit system rather than a flat per-seat subscription. New users get a free trial of 50 credits with no credit card required. The Starter plan costs 29 dollars per month for 500 credits when billed monthly, dropping to roughly 26 dollars per month, or 6,000 credits per year, on annual billing. The Pro plan costs 55 dollars per month for 1,000 credits monthly, or roughly 49 dollars per month, or 12,000 credits per year, billed annually. Above that, FullEnrich offers custom Enterprise and agency pricing for high-volume teams, which pricing analysis has suggested can scale up to tens of thousands of dollars per month for very large operations. Credit consumption is tiered by data type: finding a verified work email typically costs 1 credit, a personal email costs 3 credits, and a verified mobile phone number costs 10 credits, reflecting the relative cost and difficulty of sourcing each data type. Unused credits roll over for three months on monthly plans and twelve months on annual plans, and every plan, including the free trial, includes unlimited user seats at no additional cost, a notable departure from the per-seat pricing common among CRM and sales tools.

FullEnrich is aimed primarily at outbound sales development teams, growth and demand generation marketers, recruiters, and revenue operations professionals who need to convert a list of names, companies, or LinkedIn profiles into verified, usable contact information at scale. Its unlimited-seat pricing model makes it particularly attractive to agencies and larger sales organizations that would otherwise pay significant per-user fees with traditional enrichment vendors, since an entire team can share one credit pool without incurring additional licensing costs per person added.

FullEnrich competes with both single-source data providers such as ZoomInfo, Lusha, and Cognism, and with newer waterfall-style enrichment tools such as Clay, which itself often incorporates FullEnrich or similar providers as one waterfall step, and Apollo.io, which combines its own database with enrichment and outbound sequencing in one platform. FullEnrich's core differentiation is architectural: rather than building and maintaining its own proprietary contact database, which requires enormous ongoing data collection and verification investment, it focuses purely on the orchestration and verification layer across many third-party sources, arguing this produces a higher find rate than any single database can achieve alone. This positions FullEnrich less as a data-owner competitor to ZoomInfo and more as a complementary or substitute layer that sales teams can plug into existing CRM and outreach workflows.

As a two-year-old, seed-stage company, FullEnrich occupies a growing niche within the broader sales intelligence and data enrichment market, one that has seen significant venture investment and product innovation as outbound sales teams look to reduce reliance on any single data vendor. Its usage-based, pay-only-for-results pricing and unlimited-seats model reflect a broader shift in the category toward consumption-based pricing over legacy per-seat subscription models, and its rapid seed funding and employee growth suggest it has found meaningful product-market fit among sales teams frustrated with the accuracy limitations of single-source enrichment tools.

enrichment.short_description: FullStory is a digital experience intelligence platform that captures every user session on a website or app, offering session replay, heatmaps, and AI-powered analytics to help product and engineering teams find and fix experience issues.
enrichment.starting_price: Free plan (30,000 sessions/month); paid plans from about $199/month
enrichment.seo_title: FullStory Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: FullStory review 2026: session replay and digital experience features, pricing tiers, pros and cons, and how it compares to Hotjar and Amplitude.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
FullStory is a digital experience intelligence platform built around the idea that every user interaction on a website or app should be captured, indexed, and searchable after the fact, rather than sampled or aggregated into anonymous metrics alone. The company was founded in 2014 in Atlanta, Georgia by Scott Voigt, Joel Webber, and Bruce Johnson, a team with prior experience at enterprise software companies who set out to solve a problem familiar to product and engineering teams: traditional web analytics tools could tell you that a metric like conversion rate had dropped, but not why, and support teams often had no way to see exactly what a frustrated customer experienced. FullStory's answer was to record every session, every click, rage click, scroll, form interaction, and error, index it, and make it retroactively searchable, so a team could ask a question like show me every user who rage-clicked on the checkout button after last Tuesday's deploy, even though nobody had thought to instrument that specific question in advance.

Over its history, FullStory has raised approximately 196 million dollars in funding from investors including Google Ventures (GV), Permira, and Salesforce Ventures, reaching a valuation of 1.8 billion dollars, making it one of the higher-profile privately held companies in the digital experience analytics space. The company is headquartered in Atlanta and has grown to roughly 500 to 600 employees, with estimated annual recurring revenue in the range of 90 to 100 million dollars as of the mid-2020s. FullStory has used its funding to expand from its original session replay product into a broader digital experience intelligence platform, adding AI-driven analysis, deeper e-commerce and mobile app support, and expanded integrations with the wider martech and product analytics stack.

Architecturally, FullStory operates by embedding a lightweight JavaScript snippet, and corresponding native SDKs for iOS and Android, into a website or app, which captures DOM-level interaction data client-side and streams it to FullStory's backend for indexing. Because FullStory indexes structural and interaction data rather than just pixels, sessions can be searched and filtered after the fact using structured queries, for example, filtering for all sessions where a specific error occurred, a specific element was rage-clicked, or a user abandoned a specific form field, without needing to have pre-defined that event as a tracked metric beforehand. This retroactive searchability is FullStory's core technical differentiator relative to simpler session-recording tools. The platform layers additional capabilities on top of raw session capture: heatmaps and click maps, conversion funnels, frustration signal detection (rage clicks, dead clicks, error clicks, and thrashed cursor movement), and, more recently, an AI feature called StoryAI Session Summaries that automatically analyzes recorded sessions and produces plain-language summaries of what happened and why, reducing the amount of manual session review needed to find a root cause. FullStory integrates with common product, analytics, support, and collaboration tools including Segment, Amplitude, Google Analytics, Slack, Zendesk, Salesforce, and Jira, letting teams pipe session context into their existing workflows rather than treating FullStory as an isolated silo.

FullStory's pricing is structured around monthly session volume rather than a flat per-seat fee, and the company publishes four broad tiers. A free plan includes 30,000 sessions per month, 12 months of data retention, and access to core capabilities including session replay, basic analytics, and debugging tools for up to 10 users, making it usable for smaller sites or teams evaluating the product. The Business tier is listed starting around 199 dollars per month on annual commitments, though real-world customer-reported costs range from roughly 199 to 750 dollars per month depending on negotiated terms and session volume. An Advanced tier, which adds the StoryAI intelligence layer and more sophisticated analytics, starts around 499 dollars per month on annual billing, with reported real-world pricing extending well past 1,000 dollars per month at higher volumes. FullStory does not publish Enterprise pricing; those contracts are negotiated individually based on headcount, session volume, and contract length. Third-party pricing benchmarking data suggests the average small-to-midsize business customer pays close to 30,000 dollars per year, while the average enterprise customer pays roughly 80,000 dollars per year, with both figures reported to be trending upward year over year.

FullStory's customers span product managers, UX researchers, front-end engineers, and customer support and success teams, and the platform is used both proactively, to identify friction points in a signup or checkout flow before they show up as a support ticket, and reactively, to reconstruct exactly what a user experienced when investigating a bug report or a spike in customer complaints. Because pricing is session-based rather than seat-based, FullStory tends to be adopted more readily by mid-market and larger digital products with meaningful traffic volume, where the cost of session capture is justified by the value of diagnosing conversion and reliability problems at scale, rather than by very small sites with minimal traffic.

FullStory operates in a competitive field that includes Hotjar, which prioritizes simplicity, breadth of lightweight feedback tools like heatmaps and surveys, and a lower price point; Microsoft Clarity, a free session-replay tool that competes primarily on cost; Smartlook, Mouseflow, and LogRocket, which target more developer-centric debugging use cases; and broader product analytics platforms like Amplitude, Mixpanel, and Heap, which focus on event-based funnel and retention analysis rather than pixel-level session replay, though Amplitude in particular has moved to unify behavioral analytics with session replay and experimentation on a single platform, a direct challenge to FullStory's positioning. FullStory's core differentiation within this landscape remains its retroactive, fully indexed searchability of session data, letting teams ask new questions of historical data without having needed to instrument tracking for that exact question in advance, together with its more recent AI summarization layer aimed at reducing the manual effort of session review.

With a 1.8 billion dollar valuation, roughly 200 million dollars raised, and an established base of enterprise and mid-market customers, FullStory is one of the more mature and well-capitalized players in the digital experience intelligence category, sitting above lower-cost tools like Hotjar and Microsoft Clarity in price and sophistication, while competing directly with Amplitude and Contentsquare for larger, analytics-sophisticated organizations. Its session-based pricing model, a four-tier structure spanning a genuinely usable free plan through negotiated enterprise contracts, and continued investment in AI-assisted analysis reflect a strategy of defending its position as organizations increasingly expect both quantitative analytics and qualitative session-level context in a single platform.

enrichment.short_description: Funkwhale is a free, open-source, self-hosted audio streaming server that lets individuals and communities run their own federated music and podcast platform connected to the wider Fediverse via ActivityPub.
enrichment.seo_title: Funkwhale Review 2026: Features, Self-Hosting and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Funkwhale review: features of this open-source, federated, self-hosted music server, licensing, self-hosting steps, pros, cons, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Funkwhale is a free and open-source, self-hosted audio streaming server designed as a federated alternative to centralized platforms like Spotify, Bandcamp, and SoundCloud. The project was created in 2015 by a developer who at the time went by Eliot Berriot and today is known as Agate Berriot, initially conceived as a self-hosted alternative to Grooveshark, the once-popular music streaming service that had shut down after a copyright dispute. Rather than building another proprietary streaming service, Funkwhale was designed from the outset as software that anyone could install on their own server, giving individuals, communities, and organizations full ownership over their music library and listening data instead of depending on a third-party company's servers, terms of service, or continued existence.

What distinguishes Funkwhale from most self-hosted media servers is its federation model. Beginning in 2018, Funkwhale added support for ActivityPub, the same open, decentralized protocol that powers Mastodon, Pixelfed, and the rest of the Fediverse. This means separate Funkwhale installations, often called pods, run by different individuals or communities, can interconnect: users on one pod can follow artists, channels, and playlists hosted on a completely different pod, share libraries, and discover new music across server boundaries, in much the same way email works across different providers. In June 2019, project governance shifted from Agate Berriot as sole maintainer to the Funkwhale Collective, a French non-profit association formed specifically to steward the project collectively. Agate Berriot stepped back from day-to-day maintenance in November 2020, and community contributors have continued development since, releasing subsequent major versions including Funkwhale 1.1 and, more recently, a 2.0 release line that expanded federation capabilities further.

Funkwhale is released under the GNU Affero General Public License version 3 (AGPLv3), a copyleft license that specifically requires anyone who runs a modified version of the software as a network service to make their source code changes available, a common choice for federated and self-hosted server software to prevent proprietary forks from a shared codebase. The software is most commonly deployed via Docker and Docker Compose, with official documentation also covering manual installation, and is supported by several managed self-hosting platforms including YunoHost, elest.io, and etke.cc for users who want Funkwhale without managing a server themselves. The project's primary code repository has accumulated thousands of commits and dozens of tagged releases over its history, reflecting sustained, if volunteer-paced, development. Funkwhale supports uploading and organizing a personal or shared music library, publishing original audio content and podcasts through artist channels, internet radio-style continuous listening, and third-party client apps, including an official Funkwhale Android app distributed through F-Droid, alongside other community-built clients that connect to a Funkwhale pod's API.

As a community-driven open-source project rather than a venture-backed company, Funkwhale does not have conventional funding rounds, headquarters, or an employee count in the way a commercial SaaS product would. Development has been supported in part by grants from the European Union's Next Generation Internet (NGI) initiative, which funds open, decentralized internet infrastructure projects, as well as ongoing donations from users through platforms like Open Collective and Liberapay. This funding model keeps the software free to use and self-host, but also means development pace and feature velocity depend on volunteer contributor availability and the relatively modest grant and donation income the project receives, rather than a dedicated, fully staffed engineering team.

There is no license fee to use Funkwhale; the software itself is entirely free and open source. The real cost to a user or organization running Funkwhale is the infrastructure required to self-host it, typically a virtual private server, storage for the audio library, and bandwidth for streaming, plus the time required to install, configure, and maintain the service, unless a managed hosting provider is used, in which case that provider's own hosting fees apply instead of any fee to the Funkwhale project itself.

Funkwhale's audience consists primarily of privacy- and ownership-conscious music and podcast listeners, digital rights and free-software communities, university or interest-group communities wanting a shared music library, netlabels and independent musicians who want a federated, ad-free channel to publish original work, and self-hosting hobbyists already running other Fediverse or self-hosted infrastructure like Nextcloud, Mastodon, or Jellyfin who want a matching audio-focused tool. It is not designed to compete with mainstream commercial streaming catalogs, since Funkwhale pods only serve content that has been uploaded or federated to them, not a licensed catalog of major-label music.

Within the self-hosted media server space, Funkwhale's closest peers are Navidrome and the older Subsonic/Airsonic family, which focus on personal music library streaming without federation, and Jellyfin, which covers audio alongside video and photos as part of a broader self-hosted media server suite. Funkwhale's differentiator relative to all of these is its ActivityPub federation, positioning it as an audio-focused member of the same software family as Mastodon rather than a purely personal jukebox. Against commercial streaming services like Spotify or Bandcamp, Funkwhale is not a competitor in catalog size but an alternative model entirely, trading a licensed commercial catalog for full user ownership, no advertising, no algorithmic recommendation lock-in, and no risk of a service shutting down and taking a user's library with it. For a directory audience, Funkwhale is best understood as infrastructure software for people who want to run their own corner of the audio Fediverse rather than a consumer app to download and use immediately.

enrichment.short_description: Fusebox is a podcast hosting and customizable web player platform originally built by Pat Flynn as the Smart Podcast Player, offering unlimited-episode hosting, brandable players, analytics, and monetization tools for independent podcasters.
enrichment.seo_title: Fusebox Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Fusebox review: podcast hosting and player features, 2026 pricing, pros and cons, and how it compares to Buzzsprout, Libsyn, and Transistor.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Fusebox is a podcast hosting and web player platform whose roots trace back to 2015, when entrepreneur and podcaster Pat Flynn, frustrated with the podcast player plugins available for his own show, AskPat, built a custom WordPress player for himself with developer Matthew Gartland. What began as a personal tool released to the public as the Smart Podcast Player struck a chord with other podcasters looking for a more attractive, more functional way to embed episodes on their websites, and it grew into a standalone commercial product operated by SPI Labs, LLC, the software arm of Flynn's broader Smart Passive Income (SPI) media and education business. The product was later rebranded from Smart Podcast Player to Fusebox, reflecting an expanded scope beyond just a WordPress plugin toward a full podcasting toolkit.

For years, Fusebox (as Smart Podcast Player) was known specifically as a web player, a way to make episodes look and function better when embedded on a podcaster's own site, while podcasters still relied on separate services like Libsyn, Buzzsprout, or Podbean for the actual media hosting and RSS feed generation. That changed in early 2023, when Fusebox introduced its own podcast hosting, folding media hosting, RSS feed management, and its established player technology into one product. This let existing Fusebox player customers, and new customers, host their episodes with unlimited uploads and no monthly bandwidth or listen caps directly through Fusebox rather than juggling a separate hosting provider alongside their player.

Fusebox is operated by SPI Labs, LLC, headquartered in San Diego, California, the same corporate entity behind Pat Flynn's Smart Passive Income brand, one of the longest-running independent media and education businesses in the online entrepreneurship space. As a privately held, founder-operated company rather than a venture-backed startup, Fusebox does not disclose funding rounds, valuation, or detailed employee counts publicly; it operates as a bootstrapped product line within the broader SPI portfolio rather than a standalone venture-funded SaaS business.

Fusebox's feature set centers on two connected pieces: hosting and playback. On the hosting side, the product supports unlimited episode uploads with no monthly cap on hosting hours or listens, RSS feed generation and management, and support for hosting multiple podcast feeds within a single account. On the player side, Fusebox offers several distinct, brandable player formats, including a Single Track player for individual episodes, an Archive player for full episode libraries, a Sticky player that stays visible while a visitor browses a site, and a Full-Page player, each of which can be color-matched to a podcaster's brand. The platform includes a listening analytics dashboard covering downloads, listens, and audience retention; integrated transcript support through partners like Rev, Temi, ConvertKit, and Mailchimp; monetization tools supporting advertising, sponsorships, and subscription-based premium content; and API access for developers who want to build custom integrations on top of Fusebox data.

Fusebox offers two published self-serve paid plans plus a limited free option and a 14-day free trial on paid tiers. The Starter plan costs 9 dollars per month and includes podcast hosting with unlimited episode uploads, listening analytics, all four web player formats, brand color-matching for the Single Track player, the transcripts plugin, email support, and API access. The Pro plan costs 19 dollars per month and includes everything in Starter, aimed at more established podcasters focused on audience growth, though the specific incremental features distinguishing Pro from Starter beyond audience-growth positioning are not fully itemized in public marketing materials. Annual billing on either plan reduces the effective monthly cost by roughly a third compared to paying monthly. Fusebox also offers a free tier positioned for podcasters who simply want to replace an existing host's player with Fusebox's more customizable, responsive player without hosting their episodes through Fusebox itself, and separate Business and Enterprise pricing is available on request for larger media operations.

Fusebox's core audience is independent and semi-professional podcasters, particularly those who came up through the online business, content marketing, and creator-economy space that Pat Flynn himself is known for, who care as much about how their show looks and performs on their own website as about the underlying RSS distribution. Because the product started life purely as a player and only later added hosting, it tends to appeal especially to podcasters who already have or want a strong owned website presence for their show, rather than podcasters who only care about distribution to Apple Podcasts and Spotify.

In the broader podcast hosting market, Fusebox competes with established hosting-first platforms including Buzzsprout, Libsyn, Transistor, Podbean, and Captivate, most of which built their reputation on hosting and RSS management first and added web players as a secondary feature. Fusebox's positioning is effectively inverted: its player technology, refined over nearly a decade as the Smart Podcast Player, was and remains the core differentiator, with hosting added as a natural extension so customers no longer need to pay for and configure a separate host alongside their player. This makes Fusebox a particularly natural fit for podcasters who were already using the Smart Podcast Player for its embeddable, brandable player experience and wanted to consolidate their hosting under the same account, while podcasters prioritizing pure hosting features, dynamic ad insertion at scale, or enterprise-grade analytics may still find larger dedicated hosting platforms more full-featured.

enrichment.short_description: FusionAuth is a customer identity and access management platform that can be self-hosted or run in the cloud, offering authentication, authorization, SSO, MFA, and user management with a free Community edition and flat-rate paid pricing.

enrichment.seo_title: FusionAuth Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: FusionAuth review 2026: self-hosted and cloud CIAM features, pricing tiers, pros and cons, and how it compares to Auth0, Okta, and Keycloak.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
FusionAuth is a customer identity and access management (CIAM) platform built by Brian Pontarelli, a software engineer and entrepreneur who had previously built identity and user-management tooling under his earlier company, Inversoft, including a product called Passport that launched around 2015. FusionAuth emerged as the successor and rebrand of that earlier identity work, with the company most commonly citing 2018 as its founding year for the FusionAuth product and brand as it exists today. The company is headquartered in Broomfield, Colorado, in the Denver metropolitan area, and has grown into a recognized independent alternative in a CIAM market historically dominated by Okta and Auth0 as well as the open-source Keycloak project.

Unlike many identity startups that raise early and often, FusionAuth operated largely as a bootstrapped, founder-led business for most of its history, reportedly reaching around 10 million dollars in annual recurring revenue without significant outside capital. The company took on its first major institutional investment in November 2023, a 65 million dollar growth round from growth equity firm Updata Partners, capital the company has used to expand its go-to-market and engineering teams while maintaining its self-hosting-friendly product philosophy. FusionAuth has been recognized on the Inc. 5000 list of fastest-growing private US companies in multiple recent years, and employs somewhere in the range of roughly 50 to 100 people depending on the data source, reflecting continued steady growth.

Technically, FusionAuth is a downloadable, self-hostable identity server, built primarily on the Java Virtual Machine, that exposes a full REST API alongside official client libraries for numerous languages, and can be deployed self-hosted on a company's own infrastructure, in a dedicated managed instance, on-premises, in hybrid configurations, or fully air-gapped for regulated or high-security environments, in addition to being available as a FusionAuth-hosted cloud service. This deployment flexibility, particularly the ability to run entirely on infrastructure the customer controls, is one of FusionAuth's clearest differentiators from purely SaaS-only CIAM vendors like Auth0 or Frontegg. Functionally, FusionAuth supports the full range of modern identity standards, including OAuth2, OpenID Connect, SAML v2, LDAP, and SCIM, along with branded hosted login pages, social login, single sign-on across multiple applications, multi-factor authentication, biometric authentication via passkeys and WebAuthn, passwordless and magic-link login, breached-password detection, and machine-to-machine authentication for service-to-service calls. A single FusionAuth instance supports true multi-tenancy, letting one deployment serve multiple customer groups, brands, or even separate staging and production environments, without requiring separate licenses or infrastructure for each.

FusionAuth's pricing is structured to keep its core identity server genuinely free for unlimited users when self-hosted: the Community edition costs nothing and includes the core authentication standards along with community support through forums, Slack, and GitHub. Beyond that free tier, FusionAuth offers paid Starter, Essentials, and Enterprise plans available either self-hosted or as a FusionAuth-managed cloud deployment. The Starter plan is the entry paid tier, with pricing beginning around 162 dollars per month on annual billing, adding premium authentication features such as advanced multi-factor authentication, application theming, breached password detection, and machine-to-machine capabilities. Essentials and Enterprise sit above Starter and add advanced connectivity and security features, custom OAuth scopes, dedicated support (including 24/7 phone and email support and a private Slack channel at the Enterprise level), advanced threat detection, an SSO Tenant Manager, and high-availability hosting backed by a 99.99 percent uptime SLA; third-party pricing trackers have reported figures in the range of roughly 790 to nearly 3,000 dollars per month for these upper tiers, though FusionAuth's own published starting figures and independent benchmarks do not always agree exactly, so prospective buyers should confirm current numbers directly with FusionAuth given the platform's flexible, usage-influenced quoting. A key selling point across all paid tiers is that FusionAuth does not charge unpredictable per-monthly-active-user fees the way many SaaS-only CIAM vendors do, instead offering flatter, more predictable pricing regardless of how many identity providers or connections a customer configures.

FusionAuth's target customers are engineering teams, particularly at companies with existing Java, or more broadly JVM- and API-centric, technology stacks, who want either full control over where their identity data lives for compliance or data-sovereignty reasons, or a lower, more predictable total cost of ownership than MAU-based SaaS identity vendors as their user base scales. This makes it particularly attractive to regulated industries such as healthcare, government, and financial services, where self-hosting or air-gapped deployment can be a hard requirement, as well as to cost-conscious engineering teams at high-growth consumer or B2B products who have run the numbers on what an Auth0-style per-MAU pricing model would cost them at scale.

FusionAuth's most direct point of comparison is Keycloak, the long-standing open-source identity provider originally from Red Hat, which FusionAuth explicitly positions itself against as a more modern, better-documented, and commercially supported alternative for teams that find Keycloak difficult to operate or extend. Against SaaS-first competitors like Auth0, Frontegg, WorkOS, and Clerk, FusionAuth's differentiation is its self-hosting flexibility and flat-rate rather than per-MAU pricing, which can make it significantly cheaper at scale for products with large user bases, even though it typically requires more hands-on infrastructure management than a fully hosted SaaS alternative.

With roughly 65 million dollars in outside funding secured relatively late in its life after years of bootstrapped growth, consistent recognition on fast-growth company lists, and a product architecture built explicitly around deployment flexibility and predictable pricing, FusionAuth occupies a distinct niche in the CIAM market: it is neither a pure open-source community project like Keycloak nor a SaaS-only vendor like Auth0, but a commercially supported product that can be run either way. This positions it well for infrastructure-conscious engineering teams and regulated industries that need identity software they can fully own and audit, while still wanting professional support and a modern feature set comparable to fully managed alternatives.

faqs.answer: Yes, FusionAuth's Community edition is free for unlimited users when self-hosted and includes core authentication standards with community support; paid Starter, Essentials, and Enterprise tiers add premium features and support.

faqs.question: Can FusionAuth be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, FusionAuth can be deployed self-hosted, on-premises, in hybrid configurations, or fully air-gapped, in addition to being available as a FusionAuth-managed cloud service.

faqs.question: Who founded FusionAuth?
faqs.answer: FusionAuth was founded by Brian Pontarelli, building on identity technology he had previously developed at his earlier company Inversoft, with FusionAuth as a distinct product and brand generally dated to 2018.

faqs.question: How does FusionAuth pricing compare to Auth0?
faqs.answer: FusionAuth uses flatter, more predictable pricing rather than charging per monthly active user, which can make it significantly less expensive than Auth0-style SaaS pricing as a company's user base grows large.

faqs.question: What identity standards does FusionAuth support?
faqs.answer: FusionAuth supports OAuth2, OpenID Connect, SAML v2, LDAP, and SCIM, along with multi-factor authentication, passkeys and WebAuthn, and passwordless login.

faqs.question: Is FusionAuth a good alternative to Keycloak?
faqs.answer: Many teams evaluate FusionAuth against Keycloak because both can be self-hosted; FusionAuth positions itself as a more modern, better-documented, and commercially supported option for teams that find Keycloak difficult to operate or extend.

enrichment.short_description: FusionPBX is a free, open-source, multi-tenant PBX and voice-switch web GUI for FreeSWITCH, used to run carrier-grade phone systems, call centers, and white-label VoIP hosting platforms.

enrichment.seo_title: FusionPBX Review 2026: Features, Self-Hosting and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: FusionPBX review: open-source multi-tenant PBX features built on FreeSWITCH, self-hosting guide, pros, cons, and comparison to FreePBX.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
FusionPBX is a free, open-source web-based graphical user interface for FreeSWITCH, the widely used open-source telephony engine, that turns FreeSWITCH's powerful but low-level switching core into a full-featured, administrable multi-tenant PBX (private branch exchange) and voice-switching platform. The project traces its first commits back to 2012 and represents the ongoing open-source effort of Mark J. Crane, who created and has continued to steward FusionPBX as it grew from a personal project into a widely deployed piece of telecom infrastructure. Over more than a decade of development the project has accumulated tens of thousands of commits from hundreds of contributors, reflecting sustained community and commercial interest in having a capable, free alternative to proprietary or single-tenant PBX software.

Architecturally, FusionPBX sits on top of FreeSWITCH, a highly scalable, multi-platform communications engine originally designed to provide carrier-grade switching and telephony features, and adds the web-based administration layer, domain-based multi-tenancy, provisioning tooling, and billing hooks that FreeSWITCH itself does not provide out of the box. Its defining architectural feature is true domain-based multi-tenancy: a single FusionPBX installation can host many separate customer organizations, each fully isolated with its own dialplan, extensions, users, and configuration, which is what makes FusionPBX popular not just for internal company phone systems but as the underlying platform for VoIP carriers, hosted PBX resellers, and white-label UCaaS (unified communications as a service) providers who need to serve many end-customers from one back end.

Feature-wise, FusionPBX provides unlimited extensions, voicemail with voicemail-to-email delivery, music on hold, call parking, ring groups, interactive voice response (IVR) menus, automatic call distribution and call queues for call center use cases, fax handling, conference bridging, device auto-provisioning for a wide range of SIP hardphones, and a white-label-capable reseller and billing framework. Because it can function as a call center server, fax server, conferencing server, and voice application platform in addition to a standard PBX, organizations often deploy FusionPBX as a single consolidated voice infrastructure layer rather than stitching together multiple separate telephony tools.

FusionPBX's source code is distributed under the Mozilla Public License 1.1 (MPL 1.1), a permissive-leaning open-source license, and is hosted on GitHub under the FusionPBX organization, with the primary repository at github.com/fusionpbx/fusionpbx. Contributors are asked to sign a Contributor License Agreement (CLA) before their changes are accepted into the core project, a common governance mechanism for open-source projects that also offer paid commercial support or dual-licensing options. As with many infrastructure-grade open-source telecom projects, FusionPBX has an active ecosystem of third-party consultancies and hosting providers who offer paid installation, customization, managed hosting, and support services around the free core software, even though the FusionPBX project itself does not charge licensing fees.

There is no cost to download, install, and run FusionPBX; it is entirely free, open-source software, and organizations are free to self-host it on their own servers, virtual machines, or cloud infrastructure without any per-extension, per-tenant, or per-user licensing fee from the project itself. The practical costs involved are the server infrastructure required to run FreeSWITCH and FusionPBX reliably, which can range from a small VPS for a handful of extensions to substantial dedicated hardware for carrier-scale multi-tenant deployments, SIP trunking or carrier connectivity fees paid to a telephony provider, and, optionally, fees paid to third-party consultancies or managed hosting providers for installation, customization, or ongoing support, none of which flow back to the FusionPBX project itself.

FusionPBX's audience spans a wide range of technical sophistication: on one end, IT administrators and telecom hobbyists deploying a single-tenant office phone system as a free alternative to a commercial hosted PBX subscription, and on the other end, VoIP carriers, hosted PBX resellers, and UCaaS providers building a multi-tenant, white-label phone system product for hundreds or thousands of end-customer businesses on top of FusionPBX's domain isolation and billing framework. Call centers also use FusionPBX specifically for its automatic call distribution and queueing capabilities, layering a call center reporting and wallboard experience, available through some paid support or membership offerings around the open-source core, on top of the base PBX functionality.

FusionPBX's closest and most frequently compared alternative is FreePBX, the other major open-source web GUI for telephony, which runs on the Asterisk engine rather than FreeSWITCH and is generally considered better suited to single-tenant or small-to-medium business deployments. FusionPBX's advantage in the eyes of carriers and hosted-PBX resellers is its true multi-tenant architecture and FreeSWITCH's underlying scalability, which make it the more common choice for organizations that need to serve many independent customer domains from one platform rather than run one phone system for one organization. Beyond FreePBX, FusionPBX also competes indirectly with proprietary hosted PBX and UCaaS platforms such as RingCentral, 8x8, and 3CX, though those are commercial SaaS products rather than open-source software a customer runs themselves; FusionPBX's core appeal against all of them remains the combination of zero licensing cost, full source-code control, and carrier-grade multi-tenancy that most proprietary or single-tenant alternatives do not offer together in one free package.

enrichment.short_description: Gainsight is an enterprise customer success and product experience platform that helps subscription businesses manage customer health, onboarding, renewals, and expansion through a unified customer data and workflow system.
enrichment.seo_title: Gainsight Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Gainsight review 2026: customer success platform features, pricing tiers, pros and cons, and how it compares to ChurnZero, Totango, and Vitally.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Gainsight is an enterprise customer success management platform whose origins trace back to 2009, when Jim Eberlin and Sreedhar Peddineni founded a company called JBara Software to help subscription businesses track and manage customer health and retention. In 2013, the company rebranded as Gainsight after bringing on Nick Mehta as CEO; Mehta, who had previously sold his startup LiveOffice to Symantec, is widely credited with turning Gainsight from a small engineering-heavy team of roughly a dozen people into one of the companies most associated with popularizing customer success as a distinct business discipline and job function within SaaS organizations. Gainsight is headquartered in San Francisco, California, and the founding team, together with Mehta's leadership, positioned the product not just as software but as part of a broader movement to formalize customer success management as a profession, complete with its own conferences (the Pulse conference series), certifications, and community, alongside the software product itself.

Over its life as an independent, venture-backed company, Gainsight raised roughly 187 million dollars across seven funding rounds from nineteen-plus investors, including a widely reported 52 million dollar Series E round in 2017 with participation from Lightspeed Venture Partners and Cisco Investments, a round that helped push Gainsight toward a valuation of 1.1 billion dollars, making it one of the first companies to reach unicorn status specifically within the customer success software category. In November 2020, Vista Equity Partners acquired a majority stake in Gainsight in a deal that valued the company at 1.1 billion dollars, transitioning Gainsight from an independently venture-backed company to a portfolio company of one of the largest enterprise software-focused private equity firms. Since the Vista acquisition, Gainsight has continued to grow both organically and through acquisitions, including inSided, a customer community and product feedback platform, expanding its footprint from pure customer success management into a broader customer experience and product-led growth suite. Gainsight today employs somewhere in the range of 1,000 to 1,200 people globally, with offices across the US, UK, Netherlands, Israel, and India, reflecting its maturation into a genuinely large, global enterprise software vendor.

Functionally, Gainsight's platform is organized around a unified customer data layer, commonly referred to as Customer 360, that pulls together product usage data, support ticket history, billing and contract information, survey responses, and manual interaction notes into a single view of each customer account. On top of that data layer, Gainsight offers configurable health Scorecards that combine multiple weighted signals into a single customer health score, often enhanced with machine-learning-based predictive scoring; a Timeline feature for logging and tracking customer interactions like calls, emails, and meetings; automated Playbooks and Journey Orchestration tools that trigger specific actions, alerts, or outreach sequences when a customer's health score or usage pattern crosses a defined threshold; renewal forecasting and expansion or upsell opportunity tracking tied into CRM systems, most commonly Salesforce; organizational relationship mapping to track stakeholders within a customer account; and, following Gainsight's acquisitions and platform expansion, digital customer journey and community or product-feedback tooling that extends the platform beyond high-touch, CSM-led engagement into lower-touch, digital-led customer success motions as well. Gainsight has also invested heavily in AI-powered productivity features intended to help customer success managers draft communications, summarize account activity, and surface risk signals automatically rather than requiring manual review of raw data.

Gainsight uses a quote-based, per-user pricing model rather than publishing fixed self-serve prices, which is typical for enterprise B2B software sold primarily through a direct sales process. Its two primary published tiers are Essentials and Enterprise. Essentials is commonly reported at around 150 dollars per user per month, translating to roughly 1,200 to 2,400 dollars per user per year depending on contract terms, and is positioned for growing mid-market SaaS companies wanting foundational customer success capabilities such as AI-assisted productivity tools, Customer 360, playbooks, dashboards, health scorecards, surveys, digital customer journeys, renewal forecasting, and organizational mapping. Enterprise is commonly reported at around 300 dollars per user per month, roughly 2,400 to 4,200 dollars per user per year, adding everything in Essentials plus advanced and custom integrations and access to dedicated enterprise customer success management support from Gainsight itself. Because pricing is quote-based and often bundled with a minimum number of licensed full users and defined customer or account limits, actual contract value varies significantly by company size; third-party pricing benchmarks put the median Gainsight customer's annual spend at roughly 50,000 dollars, with reported discounts averaging around 15 percent off list price through negotiation, and larger, more complex enterprise deployments regularly reaching well into six figures annually.

Gainsight's core buyer is a dedicated customer success or customer experience organization, most often at a mid-market to large enterprise SaaS or subscription business running Salesforce as its CRM, with at least a handful, and often twenty or more, dedicated customer success managers who need a shared system of record and workflow automation layer distinct from the sales-focused CRM itself. The platform's depth of configuration, health scoring, playbook automation, and reporting makes it powerful but also relatively complex to implement and administer, which is why it tends to be a stronger fit for organizations large enough to justify dedicated Gainsight administrators than for very small teams.

Gainsight competes most directly with ChurnZero, a real-time customer success platform built specifically to help subscription businesses proactively spot churn risk and engage customers; Totango, another established enterprise customer success platform; Vitally, which emphasizes a cleaner, faster-to-implement interface aimed at leaner, faster-growing SaaS teams; and Planhat, a newer entrant also targeting mid-market customer success teams. Reviewers and buyers commonly frame the decision as one of scale and complexity: larger enterprises running Salesforce with a mature, dedicated CS operations function tend to favor Gainsight for its depth, while smaller or leaner SaaS teams more often choose ChurnZero, Vitally, or Planhat for faster setup, a gentler learning curve, and lower total cost of ownership.

As a Vista Equity Partners-owned company valued at 1.1 billion dollars at acquisition, with roughly a thousand employees and a customer base concentrated among the larger, more mature SaaS companies that were early to invest in formal customer success organizations, Gainsight is widely regarded as the enterprise incumbent and category-defining vendor in customer success software, a position reinforced by its role in popularizing the customer success profession itself through the Pulse conference and related community programs, even as newer, leaner competitors continue to chip away at the mid-market segment with simpler, less expensive alternatives.
